title: Checkpoint: Everything From Logic Gates Through Loss Functions
concept: Reviewing the complete structural journey of deep learning foundations from logic gates and perceptrons through layers, activations, batch loops, and loss metrics.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why must the computer store intermediate activations during the forward pass?

options.text: Because backpropagation requires those activation values to calculate weight gradients.
To save them permanently to disk.

exercises.explain: Correct! Backpropagation math requires the forward activation values to compute gradients.

cards.title: The milestones behind you
cards.body: Look back at the path you've climbed. You started with the simplest switches (logic gates), built a single learning neuron (perceptron), saw where it broke (XOR), stacked them into layers, introduced curves (activations), optimized them (batches/epochs), and measured their errors (loss). You have built the entire forward half of deep learning!

cards.title: The Structural Checkpoint
cards.body: Let's review the milestones: 1. **Logic Gates & Perceptrons** (binary inputs and threshold decisions), 2. **XOR Problem** (exposed linear limits, solved by hidden layers), 3. **Activations** (ReLU, Sigmoid, Softmax squashing), 4. **Forward Pass** (layer transformations), 5. **Training Loop & Loss** (BCE, CCE, MSE, batches, epochs, early stopping).

cards.title: The complete arch bridge
cards.body: Think of the forward pipeline like building a stone arch bridge. Logic gates and perceptrons are the individual stones. Stacking hidden layers forms the curve of the arch. Activation functions are the mortar holding them together. The loss node at the end is the keystone locking the bridge in place. You can now walk all the way across to the other side.

cards.title: Connecting the concepts
cards.body: When you use a modern app (like a voice assistant), it is running this exact pipeline: audio signals are loaded as inputs, processed in parallel via weight matrices, activated by ReLU, squashed to probabilities via Softmax, and checked against target distributions. The magic is just this chain of simple concepts!

cards.title: Deep learning is not a mystery
cards.body: It's common to hear people refer to deep learning as an inexplicable mystery. While the millions of weights are hard to read, the *structure* of how a network makes decisions is a clean, logical sequence of step-by-step math. You now understand exactly how the black box is constructed.

cards.title: Deep Learning Foundations Checkpoint
cards.body: The core phases you have conquered:

left.text:
Logic Gates & Perceptrons
The XOR linear limit
Hidden Layers & Deep Networks

right.text:
Activations (ReLU, Softmax)
Epochs, Batches & Shuffling
Classification & Regression Loss

exercises.prompt: Which of these concepts explains why neural networks need hidden layers?

options.text: To solve non-linear problems like XOR by bending the decision boundary.
To speed up file serialization times.
To convert classifications into regression tasks.
To make the model weights completely random.

exercises.explain: Correct! Hidden layers allow networks to combine splits and learn complex, non-linear boundaries.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An activation function like ReLU is required because without non-linearity, stacking multiple hidden layers mathematically collapses into a single straight line.
exercises.explain: True! Without non-linear activation functions, a deep network is mathematically no better than a single-layer perceptron.

exercises.sentence: While batch size determines how many rows are processed in parallel, one complete pass over all rows is called an ___.
exercises.explain: An epoch represents one full pass of the training algorithm over the entire dataset.

exercises.prompt: Which loss function should you choose to train a model that predicts whether a picture is a Cat, Dog, or Bird?

options.text: Categorical Cross-Entropy loss, paired with Softmax activation.
Binary Cross-Entropy loss, paired with Sigmoid activation.
Mean Squared Error (MSE), paired with linear activation.

exercises.explain: Correct! Sorting into three or more classes is a multi-class task, requiring CCE loss and Softmax.

exercises.prompt: You have completed Day 350 and built a rock-solid foundation in the forward and loss phases of Deep Learning! What is the next logical step in training these networks?

options.text: Backpropagation (the backward pass), to calculate how to adjust the weights to reduce the loss score you just measured.
Starting over and retraining the classical linear regression model.
Deleting the loss node to speed up predictions.
Switching all models from supervised to unsupervised clustering.

exercises.explain: Absolutely correct! You've mastered how a network flows forward to make a prediction and measure its error. The next step is backpropagation — flowing that error backward to adjust the weights and make the model smarter. Congratulations on reaching Day 350 and completing the Forward Pass Capstone! You have built a powerful deep learning foundation!
